mission.title: Install and Configure MySQL Operator for Kubernetes (Oracle)
mission.description: The MySQL Operator for Kubernetes automates the management of MySQL InnoDB Cluster setups within a Kubernetes environment. It simplifies tasks such as deployment, scaling, and backup, making it ideal for users who need a robust database solution in a cloud-native architecture. Key trade-offs include the need for proper configuration and monitoring to ensure optimal performance and reliability.

steps.title: Add the MySQL Operator Helm Repository
steps.description:
This step adds the official MySQL Operator Helm repository to your Helm configuration, allowing you to install the operator easily.

```bash

helm repo add mysql-operator https://mysql.github.io/mysql-operator/

helm repo update

```

> This command adds the MySQL Operator repository.
> Updates the local Helm chart repository cache.

steps.title: Install the MySQL Operator
steps.description:
This command installs the MySQL Operator in the 'mysql-operator' namespace, creating the namespace if it does not exist.

```bash

helm install mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --create-namespace --version 9.0.0

```

> Replace '9.0.0' with the desired version if needed.

steps.title: Verify the Operator Installation
steps.description:
Check if the MySQL Operator is running correctly by querying the deployment status in the specified namespace.

```bash

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> You should see the operator deployment with a READY status.

steps.title: Create MySQL Root User Secret
steps.description:
This step creates a Kubernetes secret that contains the credentials for the MySQL root user, which is necessary for managing the MySQL InnoDB Cluster.

```bash

kubectl create secret generic mypwds --from-literal=rootUser=root --from-literal=rootHost=% --from-literal=rootPassword: <REDACTED>'

```

> Ensure to replace 'sakila' with a secure password.

steps.title: Define and Create MySQL InnoDB Cluster
steps.description:
This step defines a MySQL InnoDB Cluster resource using a YAML manifest and applies it to the cluster.

```bash

kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.28'
EOF

```

> Adjust the version as needed.
commands:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.28'
EOF

steps.title: Verify MySQL InnoDB Cluster Creation
steps.description:
Check the status of the MySQL InnoDB Cluster to ensure it has been created successfully.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com mycluster

```

> You should see the cluster status and details.

uninstall.title: Uninstall the MySQL Operator
uninstall.description:
This step removes the MySQL Operator and its associated resources. Be aware that this may lead to data loss if not handled properly.

```bash

helm uninstall mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator

kubectl delete namespace mysql-operator

```

> This command removes the operator from the specified namespace.
> This command deletes the namespace and all resources within it.

upgrade.title: Upgrade the MySQL Operator
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, ensure you have a backup of your data. This step will upgrade the MySQL Operator to a new version.

```bash

kubectl get innodbclusters.mysql.oracle.com -o yaml > backup-innodbcluster.yaml

helm upgrade mysql-operator mysql-operator/mysql-operator --namespace mysql-operator --version 9.0.1

kubectl get deployment -n mysql-operator mysql-operator

```

> Backup the current InnoDB Cluster configuration.
> Replace '9.0.1' with the desired version.
> Verify the operator is running with the new version.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Failed to create InnoDB Cluster: MySQL server is not reachable'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL server may not be running or is misconfigured.

**Fix:**
Check the MySQL Operator logs and ensure the MySQL server is running correctly.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Secret mypwds not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The secret containing MySQL credentials has not been created or is in a different namespace.

**Fix:**
Ensure the secret is created in the correct namespace using the command: kubectl create secret generic mypwds ...

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'InnoDB Cluster is not ready'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The MySQL pods may not be in a running state due to resource constraints.

**Fix:**
Check pod statuses with kubectl get pods -n mysql-operator and ensure sufficient resources are allocated.

troubleshooting.title: Error: 'Helm release mysql-operator not found'
troubleshooting.description:
**Cause:** The Helm release may not have been installed or has been deleted.

**Fix:**
Reinstall the MySQL Operator using the Helm install command.

versionNotes.changes: Introduced support for MySQL 8.0.28, enhanced backup capabilities, and improved error handling.
versionNotes.migrationSteps: If upgrading from version 8.4, ensure to review the new configuration options in the documentation.

resolution.summary: You should see the MySQL InnoDB Cluster status as 'Ready' when everything is working correctly, with the command output showing the cluster details.
codeSnippets:
kubectl apply -f - <<EOF
apiVersion: mysql.oracle.com/v2
kind: InnoDBCluster
metadata:
  name: mycluster
spec:
  secretName: mypwds
  version: '8.0.28'
EOF

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running with the necessary resources.
